lead: A moving day in Felixstowe has a couple of quirks all its own, from the seafront parking to the port traffic that can build on the approach roads, and getting ahead of them is what keeps the day calm. We've run countless Felixstowe moves from our Westerfield base, and the smoothest ones always come down to the same handful of well-timed jobs. This checklist pulls together everything we'd want a Felixstowe customer to know, so your move-in day by the sea goes exactly to plan.

sections.h: The Week Before: Lay the Groundwork
sections.body:
The calmest Felixstowe moving days are built in the week beforehand. By now your packing should be nearly done, with only the kitchen and bathroom essentials left for the final morning, and every box labelled on the side as well as the top with its room and a note of the contents. That one habit transforms unloading day, especially if you are moving into a period home near the front where carrying boxes up and down stairs is part of the job. Do a last check of the loft, shed and garage now rather than on the morning itself.

Use this week to lock down the practical arrangements too. Confirm the crew's arrival time, make sure someone will be at both properties, and sort out the keys for handover. Defrost the freezer a couple of days ahead so it travels dry, run down the fridge contents, and if you can, arrange for pets and young children to be looked after on the day, since an empty house is safer and quicker to load.

Finally, pack an essentials box that rides with you in the car rather than the van. Kettle, mugs, tea, phone chargers, snacks, toilet roll, medicines and a change of clothes will carry you through the first evening in your new Felixstowe home without having to dig through a stack of boxes after a long day.

sections.h: Parking and Access by the Seafront
sections.body:
Parking is the detail that catches people out most in Felixstowe, particularly near the seafront. The handsome period homes along and behind the front often have no driveway, and the roads can be narrow with on-street parking that fills up, especially in the warmer months when day visitors arrive. If your property sits in this kind of spot, tell us early. We can plan smaller shuttle loads where a large lorry cannot get close, and where needed you may be able to arrange a temporary parking suspension with the council.

It also pays to give your neighbours a friendly heads-up that a removal lorry will need space outside for a few hours. In a busy seafront street that small courtesy often means a cooperative kerb on the day, which makes everything quicker. If you have your own car, move it off the drive or away from your access the night before so we have room to work from first thing.

Further back from the front, on the more modern roads and developments, access is usually much easier, with driveways and wider streets. Even so, a quick photo of the parking situation sent over when you book lets us bring the right vehicle and avoid surprises. The more we know about the kerb outside both your old and new homes, the smoother the loading and unloading will be.

When the crew arrives, take a few minutes to walk us through the house. Point out anything fragile or precious, flag any items that are not coming with you so nothing is loaded by mistake, and make sure walkways are clear and rugs rolled back. In an older seafront property with a tight staircase, clearing the route in advance makes a real difference to how quickly and safely we can carry heavy furniture down to the van.

Keep the kettle and a few mugs to hand until the last moment, as a brew keeps everyone going on a busy morning. If you have children or pets, settle them somewhere safe and out from underfoot so the team can move freely. We protect floors and doorways as we go, but an uncluttered space is always the best protection, and it lets the loading flow without stop-start delays.

As the van fills, keep a mental tally of the big items, especially anything from the garden, the shed or a summerhouse, which are easy to overlook in a coastal property. It is far simpler to notice something has not been loaded while we are still on site than to discover it once we have pulled away. Pause before you lock up and let yourself register that the hardest part of the day is already behind you.

sections.h: Routes, Port Traffic and the Final Sweep
sections.body:
Felixstowe's roads have one peculiarity worth planning around: the freight heading to and from the country's largest container port can build up at certain times of day on the main approaches. When we plan your move we factor this in, choosing timings and routes that keep your belongings moving rather than crawling behind a line of lorries. If you are following in your own car, there is no need to keep us in sight; just head to the new place and we will meet you there.

Before you leave the old house for good, do a thorough final sweep with us. Check every cupboard, behind every door, the loft, the garden, the shed and any seafront storage, because it is remarkable what gets left in the last rush. Take and photograph your meter readings, lock all the windows, and make sure you have the keys for the new property and a clear plan for handing over the old ones. Five careful minutes here saves a frustrating trip back across town tomorrow.

Leaving the house clean and the rubbish dealt with is both a kindness to whoever follows and a good way to part on friendly terms. Once the door is locked behind you, the old chapter is closed and your Felixstowe future begins, just a short drive down the coast road away.

sections.h: Arriving and Unloading at the New Home
sections.body:
At the new Felixstowe house, all that careful labelling earns its keep. As we bring each box in we place it in its room according to the label, turning the days ahead from a treasure hunt into a tidy tick-list. Find yourself a spot where you can direct the flow, pointing the crew towards the right rooms and confirming where the larger furniture should sit before it is set down, because nobody enjoys shifting a heavy wardrobe twice.

Check your inventory against what arrives, particularly if anything travelled separately or came out of storage. We will reassemble the beds and wardrobes we took apart at the old house, so your first night by the sea is a comfortable one. Make the beds, find the kettle, and resist the temptation to unpack the whole house at once; focus on the rooms you need first and let the rest wait until the morning.

Once the van is empty and you have confirmed nothing is left aboard, the move is done. Locate the stopcock and the fuse board, test the heating, and then step outside for a moment of sea air to mark the occasion. A well-run Felixstowe move should end with you tired but settled, ready to enjoy the town rather than buried under a mountain of unopened boxes.

Not every Felixstowe move runs neatly from door to door in a single day. Chains slip, completion dates move, and sometimes the new place is not ready when you have to leave the old one. This is where secure storage quietly smooths the way. We've helped many people moving to Felixstowe bridge an awkward gap of days or weeks by keeping their belongings safe and delivering when the new home is finally theirs, taking the pressure off an uncertain chain.

If there is any chance you might need it, raise storage when you first get in touch rather than at the last minute. That lets us plan the whole move as one smooth operation, packing and labelling everything as if it were a normal move and setting aside what you will need to reach in the meantime. It is far calmer than trying to force an unsettled move into one hectic day.

Storage also suits the gentler reasons that come up around a coastal move: downsizing to a smaller seaside home and needing somewhere for furniture while you decide, renovating before you fully move in, or simply wanting breathing space. In every case, a secure place to keep your things means you move into your new Felixstowe home properly and on your own terms, which after two decades of doing this is exactly the outcome we aim for.

faqs: How do I sort parking for a removal lorry near the Felixstowe seafront?

Seafront and period streets often have narrow roads and busy on-street parking, especially in summer. You may be able to arrange a temporary parking suspension with the council, and where a lorry can't get close we plan smaller shuttle loads. Tell us your Felixstowe address early so we can advise.
Should I plan my Felixstowe move around port traffic?

It helps. Freight to and from the container port can build on the main approach roads at certain times, so we plan timings and routes for your Felixstowe move to keep things flowing. We've moved here for twenty years and know when and where the queues form.
What goes in the essentials box for a Felixstowe move?

Pack a box that travels in your car with the kettle, mugs, tea, phone chargers, snacks, toilet roll, medicines and a change of clothes. It means your first evening in your new Felixstowe home is comfortable without unpacking a stack of boxes after the journey.
Can you store our things if the Felixstowe completion is delayed?

Yes. Our secure storage is made for delays and gaps in the chain. We keep everything safe until your new Felixstowe home is ready and deliver when you are. Mention it when you book so we plan the whole move as one smooth operation.
